Title: Database DevOps: CD for Stateful Applications
Abstract: Running stateful applications can provide many of the same advantages as stateless applications. In this talk, Stephen will share some thoughts on managing stateful applications as part of a CD Pipeline so that applications - and the application's data - can be versioned and deployed safely and repeatedly. This talk will discuss managing structural changes to a database as part of a CD process. The talk will dive into automation approaches and tooling for managing data migrations between environments and running database migrations within a CI/CD pipeline. The talk will feature real-world examples where we discuss specific schema migrations, their possible performance impacts and downtime implications. We will demonstrate how a complex migration can be done with 0 downtime. With AI and CI/CD we can provide something better than before: A more testable, repeatable, and open way to deploy stateful applications. This talk features a practical demo of how CD tooling can empower users to automate data migrations within Kubernetes.

Title: RAG in production: challenges, techniques and solutions
Abstract: You may have seen reports of AI models suggesting eating rocks or putting glue on pizza. RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) is an architecture designed to prevent these kinds of hallucinations. RAG contains two elements: a “retriever” (vector database) to obtain contextual information, and a generator (LLM) to produce the final response. It sounds simple, but implementing and deploying RAG in production has its own challenges. In this talk, you’ll learn about some of these challenges, as well as techniques to mitigate or eliminate them.

Title: Securing Agentic AI Data Flows: Confidential MCP for Privacy-Safe Tooling in Database-Driven Systems
Abstract: As agentic AI systems increasingly interact with enterprise data platforms in healthcare, finance, and legal domains, a critical gap has emerged: large language models running inside Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) cannot safely query external databases or services without risking sensitive data exposure. While confidential compute infrastructure has matured, the data access layer remains fundamentally insecure.  This talk introduces Confidential MCP (C-MCP), a backwards-compatible extension to Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol, designed to enable auditable, privacy-preserving tool and database access for LLM agents operating in secure environments. Building on architectures such as Meta’s WhatsApp Private Processing and validated confidential computing practices, C-MCP introduces a three-zone service topology, an Anonymization Transform Layer with entropy-bounded controls, and Attested Egress Policies that govern what data can leave secure enclaves.  We will explore why traditional MCP-based integrations fail in database-centric systems, highlighting challenges in transport security, dynamic schema and tool discovery, and authentication mismatches, and how C-MCP resolves them in production-like environments.  The session also addresses operational realities, including query overreach from smaller open-source models, cumulative data leakage risks in multi-step reasoning workflows, and performance overheads when integrating secure inference with real-time data systems.  Through real-world scenarios including clinical data pipelines governed by HIPAA, contract intelligence systems handling privileged information, and AML analytics platforms subject to GLBA and GDPR, attendees will learn how to design secure, compliant, and observable AI-driven data workflows with enforceable egress controls.

Title: From Data Pipelines to AI Products: Operationalizing Machine Learning and Generative AI in Enterprise Decision Platforms
Abstract: Modern enterprises are rapidly evolving from isolated analytics initiatives to fully operational AI-driven decision platforms, but scaling these systems reliably remains a significant challenge. This session explores how organizations can bridge the gap between data engineering, MLOps, and AI productization to deliver production-grade intelligence at scale.  Drawing on real-world implementations across healthcare, marketing analytics, and technology startups, we examine how robust data pipelines, versioned datasets, and automated deployment workflows enable faster time-to-market and improved operational efficiency. The talk highlights how Database DevOps practices such as continuous integration for data, schema evolution management, and pipeline observability form the backbone of scalable AI systems.  We dive into architectures that combine machine learning and generative AI, including large language models and retrieval augmented generation, with a strong focus on reliability, reproducibility, and governance. Attendees will learn how modern data platforms support continuous model experimentation, real-time inference, and seamless integration with transactional and analytical systems.  Key areas include designing resilient cloud native data and machine learning pipelines, implementing MLOps practices for faster and safer model deployments, ensuring data quality and governance in AI workflows, enabling observability across data and model layers, and scaling AI systems for real time decision making.  This session emphasizes a practical DevOps first approach to AI, where data, models, and infrastructure are treated as versioned, testable, and deployable assets. By aligning engineering discipline with AI innovation, organizations can move beyond experimentation to build reliable and scalable AI products that deliver measurable business impact.
Slides: Bharat Kumar Reddy Karumuri - Conf42 Database DevOps 2026.pdf

Title: AI-Driven Database Tuning in DevOps: Real-World Lessons from Self-Optimizing Systems
Abstract: Database performance tuning has traditionally relied on manual intervention, static rules, and reactive troubleshooting—approaches that often struggle to keep pace with modern DevOps environments. In this session, we share practical lessons from building and deploying AI-driven database optimization systems that continuously adapt to real-world workloads.  Drawing from production case studies across finance, e-commerce, and healthcare, we explore how machine learning models were integrated into DevOps pipelines to automate indexing, query optimization, and configuration tuning. We’ll discuss what worked, what failed, and how iterative improvements led to measurable gains in performance, scalability, and operational efficiency. Attendees will gain insight into how these systems reduced manual DBA effort, improved query response times, and lowered infrastructure costs.  The talk also covers architectural decisions, including how to safely deploy self-tuning mechanisms in live environments, ensure observability, and maintain control over automated changes. We will demonstrate an open-source toolkit that analyzes query logs in real time and evolves database configurations with minimal human intervention.  This session provides actionable guidance for engineers and teams looking to integrate intelligent automation into their database DevOps workflows while avoiding common pitfalls.

Title: AI Meets SAP: Building Real Time Pricing Optimization Pipelines Beyond Rule Based Systems
Abstract: Traditional SAP pricing relies on static rules that struggle to keep up with dynamic market conditions and evolving customer behavior. As organizations adopt modern data platforms and DevOps practices, pricing remains one of the least modernized components in the enterprise stack.  This session explores how to transform pricing into a real time, data driven system using AI and modern integration patterns. We walk through a hybrid architecture where SAP handles transactional processing and financial validation, while an external AI engine fed by continuously updated data pipelines delivers optimized pricing, discount strategies, and risk adjusted recommendations.  Using API based integration and SAP BAdI extensibility, the solution maintains clean core compliance while enabling rapid iteration, model deployment, and observability aligned with Database DevOps and modern data engineering principles.  We discuss how to design real time pricing pipelines using enterprise data sources, integrate AI services with SAP without breaking core systems, and operationalize machine learning models in production with strong DevOps practices. We also address consistency, auditability, and performance at scale.  Based on real enterprise deployments, this approach significantly reduced manual interventions and enabled responsive, market aware pricing decisions. This session provides a practical blueprint for turning static ERP logic into intelligent, continuously evolving decision systems.

Title: AI-Driven Database DevOps: From Predictive Tuning to Autonomous Operations in Production
Abstract: Modern database systems are at the heart of DevOps-driven architectures, yet performance tuning, scaling, and reliability management remain highly manual and reactive in many organizations. In this talk, we share practical lessons from implementing AI-driven database optimization techniques in real-world production environments, focusing on what actually works—and what doesn’t—when integrating intelligence into database DevOps workflows.  We explore how machine learning models can be embedded into CI/CD and operational pipelines to enable predictive workload forecasting, allowing teams to proactively allocate resources and avoid performance bottlenecks during peak demand. Through concrete examples, we demonstrate how automated indexing strategies—powered by reinforcement learning—improved query performance for analytical workloads while reducing storage overhead compared to traditional approaches.  The session also dives into AI-based anomaly detection integrated with observability stacks, highlighting how teams reduced incident detection time from minutes to seconds and significantly lowered false alerts. We discuss how these improvements translated into faster debugging, more reliable deployments, and reduced operational toil.  Beyond the successes, we provide an honest look at challenges encountered, including data quality issues, integration with legacy systems, and the effort required to operationalize AI models within existing DevOps toolchains. We outline the trade-offs between automation and control, and what we would approach differently in future implementations.  Finally, we present a practical roadmap toward semi-autonomous database operations, where feedback loops continuously optimize performance with minimal human intervention. Attendees will leave with actionable insights, architecture patterns, and tooling strategies they can apply to improve database performance, scalability, and reliability in their own DevOps environments.

Title: Doing more with Cloud API's
Abstract: All the major Cloud vendors provide customers with a rich api based capability to manage your environment. In many cases these apis provide more functionality than is available through the Web console, but less easy to use. By extracting api output and loading into a database this allows you to perform complex analysis on that data, and build applications to enhance your Cloud experience

Title: Cloud-Native Data Architectures for Real-Time Marketing Analytics in Database DevOps
Abstract: Modern marketing analytics systems are increasingly constrained not by data volume, but by the ability to process, store, and act on data in real time. This talk explores how cloud-native database architectures and DevOps practices are transforming marketing analytics from batch-driven pipelines into real-time, event-driven systems capable of millisecond-level responsiveness.  Drawing from real-world implementation patterns, we examine how organizations design and operate scalable data platforms that process millions of customer events per second using distributed data stores, streaming pipelines, and cloud-managed database services. The session will highlight how teams reduced data processing latency by over 60% and improved query performance by more than 70% by adopting modern database technologies and automated infrastructure workflows.  We will walk through practical architecture decisions, including the use of data lakes vs. data warehouses, stream processing frameworks, and real-time indexing strategies for dynamic audience segmentation. Attendees will also learn how continuous integration and deployment (CI/CD) pipelines are applied to data systems, enabling seamless schema evolution, zero-downtime model retraining, and automated scaling without service disruption.  The talk will cover lessons learned from building and operating these systems in production—what worked, what failed, and how teams addressed challenges in observability, data consistency, and cost optimization. We will also explore emerging trends such as federated learning for privacy-aware analytics and AutoML integration within data pipelines.  This session is ideal for engineers and architects looking to bridge the gap between database systems and DevOps practices, with concrete examples of how to build reliable, scalable, and high-performance real-time analytics platforms.

Title: AI Assisted Azure Ops with Copilot for Diagnostics and Resource Control
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence is reshaping cloud and data platform operations by combining human expertise with intelligent automation. Copilot in Azure reflects this shift by introducing an AI powered assistant that improves resource management and diagnostic insight across complex Azure environments.  Modern cloud platforms support hundreds of services and thousands of resource types. For DevOps and database teams, managing configurations, monitoring performance, and diagnosing issues often requires deep expertise and constant context switching. Copilot simplifies this experience through natural language interaction, allowing users to explore their environments conversationally. Teams can summarize configurations, investigate performance metrics, and understand relationships between services and resources without navigating multiple tools.  Behind this capability is an architecture that combines Large Language Models with Azure control plane data, configuration metadata, and real time telemetry. This integration enables Copilot to generate context aware insights that support faster diagnostics, operational visibility, and more efficient resource management across cloud workloads, including data and database services.  The session will also highlight the role of engineers and technical advisors who contribute to Copilot’s diagnostic intelligence by validating telemetry mappings, reviewing system behavior, and building trusted troubleshooting guidance. Their input ensures that AI driven recommendations remain accurate, transparent, and operationally reliable.  Attendees will gain insight into how AI assisted operations can support DevOps and database teams by improving troubleshooting workflows, strengthening observability, and enabling more responsive cloud governance in Azure environments.
Slides: Sampath Rao Madarapu - Conf42 Database DevOps 2026.pdf

Title: Cloud-Native Core Systems: Database & DevOps for Insurance
Abstract: Insurance organizations face growing pressure to modernize legacy core platforms as regulatory demands increase and digital competitors accelerate delivery cycles. Decades-old monolithic systems—particularly tightly coupled application and database layers—limit agility, slow releases, and struggle to scale during catastrophe-driven claims spikes.  This session examines how cloud-native engineering, combined with modern database and DevOps practices, transforms core insurance systems. We explore microservices architectures that decouple data domains, containerization and Kubernetes orchestration for high availability, and Infrastructure-as-Code for reproducible, resilient database and application environments. CI/CD automation enables frequent, reliable deployments backed by automated functional, performance, and security testing.  Event-driven architectures using platforms such as Kafka or EventBridge improve resilience and reduce cascading failures, while API-first design supports secure, versioned data exchange across partner ecosystems. Modern observability—centralized logging, distributed tracing, and metrics—enables teams to diagnose incidents across distributed systems containing hundreds of services and data stores.  The session also addresses operational challenges including distributed data consistency, schema evolution, and organizational change, outlining approaches such as service mesh adoption, upskilling programs, blameless postmortems, and sustained executive commitment.  Attendees will gain a research-backed roadmap for modernizing legacy insurance platforms into scalable, resilient, DevOps-driven cloud-native systems.

Title: Schema Migrations in CI/CD: When AI Helps, When It Hurts, and How to Tell the Difference
Abstract: Schema migrations are the riskiest deploy most teams ship. Application releases can be rolled forward and back. A migration that locks a 2TB table for an hour, or quietly loses data on rollback, cannot. And now AI wants to write them for us. This talk is a working CI/CD field guide for shipping database changes safely in cloud environments, and an honest assessment of where AI-assisted review fits inside that pipeline. We'll cover the migration patterns that hold up: backward-compatible schema changes, expand-and-contract releases, automated migration tests, approval gates, and post-deploy verification. Then we'll add the AI layer where it earns its keep, including drafting forward and rollback DDL, flagging unsafe operations before review (full-table rewrites, locks held in transactions, missing concurrency hints), and triaging incidents when a migration goes sideways. I'll also be direct about where AI breaks down. Hallucinated database syntax, like Postgres flags that are actually MySQL flags. Missing the operational context that a "trivial" migration on a hot table will lock production for an hour. Rollback scripts that compile but quietly lose rows. The framework, three axes for scoring any migration workflow on blast radius, reversibility, and human-in-loop requirement, gives engineers a defensible way to decide where automation and AI belong in their pipeline, and where they don't. Attendees leave with concrete CI/CD patterns they can adopt next sprint, the specific failure modes to design around, and a practical decision rule for keeping engineering judgement in control of production data.

Title: Can a Kubernetes Operator Replace a DBA? A 30-Day Experiment
Abstract: Kubernetes operators promise to automate complex operational tasks, backups, failovers, upgrades, scaling, and turn database management into a declarative workflow. But how far does that promise actually go?  In this talk, I run a 30-day experiment to find out.  Using a PostgreSQL cluster deployed with the CloudNativePG operator, I handed over day-to-day database operations to Kubernetes and observed what happened in real-world conditions. Over the course of a month, I monitored the system while intentionally introducing common production problems: node failures, pod crashes, storage pressure, replication issues, upgrades, and backup recovery scenarios.  Some problems were resolved automatically within seconds. Others exposed surprising limitations where manual intervention was still required.  This talk walks through the experiment week by week, showing what the operator handled gracefully, what broke, and where human operators are still essential. Along the way we’ll explore the operational model behind Kubernetes operators, the trade-offs of automated database management, and the practical lessons teams should know before trusting automation with their stateful workloads.  If you’ve ever wondered whether operators can truly replace traditional database administration, or simply shift the role, this experiment provides a candid, data-driven answer.  Key Takeaways  Attendees will learn:  - How Kubernetes operators manage stateful systems like PostgreSQL - Which database failures operators can automatically recover from - Where manual operational knowledge is still required - The operational trade-offs of running databases in Kubernetes - Practical lessons from a month-long real-world experiment

Title: Prompt-Driven Microservice & Database Evolution: Using LLMs for Automated Schema Migration and DevOps Delivery
Abstract: As microservices and their underlying data systems rapidly evolve, traditional development and database change management approaches struggle to keep pace with dynamic business requirements. This session introduces a modern, prompt-driven framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to translate natural language requirements into production-ready microservice code, database schema migrations, and API integrations.  The proposed architecture integrates prompt interpretation, semantic validation, and type-safe code generation with database-aware transformation layers. It enables automated schema evolution while preserving backward compatibility, data integrity, and service reliability—key challenges in Database DevOps environments.  A core focus of this approach is aligning application and database changes within a unified DevOps pipeline. Built-in validation mechanisms detect ambiguity and enforce consistency before deployment, while automated test generation and rollback-aware migration strategies ensure safe releases in distributed systems.  This framework significantly reduces manual effort in feature delivery, accelerates release cycles, and improves collaboration between business stakeholders, developers, and data engineers. By enabling teams to define requirements in natural language and automatically generate synchronized application and database updates, it introduces a new paradigm for continuous delivery in microservice ecosystems.  Real-world implementations demonstrate measurable gains in deployment speed, system stability, and schema governance. Attendees will gain practical insights into integrating LLM-driven automation into existing DevOps pipelines, with a focus on scalable, reliable, and database-centric system evolution.

Title: Five Hard Lessons from Five Years of So-Called Serverless Databases
Abstract: After five years of managing serverless databases, I have learned that my rollercoaster journey is very similar to the CPU usage you dream of seeing in the console. This session shares five hard-earned lessons learned while working with so-called serverless databases.

Title: Ethical DevOps for AI Data Pipelines: 286% Deployment Efficiency Gains
Abstract: As AI adoption accelerates, with 83% of enterprises fast-tracking integration, aligning DevOps practices with ethical AI has become critical in database and data pipeline environments. This session focuses on how teams integrate ethical validation directly into data and database DevOps workflows, and what results they achieved in real-world systems.  We walk through practical implementations where automated ethical checks are embedded into CI/CD pipelines handling data ingestion, transformation, and model deployment. These pipelines detected 89% of potential bias issues before release while reducing validation time by 56%. We also examine continuous monitoring setups across data and model layers that improved bias detection accuracy by 79% and increased regulatory compliance by 84%.  The talk highlights architecture and tooling decisions behind cloud-scale observability systems processing over 100,000 events per second, reducing incident response time by 78% and improving early issue detection by 91%. A healthcare data platform case study shows how these practices reduced patient data privacy incidents by 67% and increased clinician trust by 79%.  Attendees will learn how to design ethical validation into database DevOps pipelines, implement governance and monitoring workflows with measurable impact, and apply automation patterns that improved deployment efficiency by 286% and overall project success rates by 75%. The session emphasizes lessons learned, trade-offs, and practical approaches that teams can adopt in their own data systems.

Title: Intentional Delivery in AI-Driven Database DevOps
Abstract: AI-assisted development is transforming Database DevOps, shifting the core constraint from “can we build it?” to “should we build it this way, and will it deliver reliable outcomes at scale?” While AI accelerates schema design, query generation, pipeline automation, and operational tasks, speed without structure can introduce inconsistency, increase data risk, and erode trust—creating what can be understood as experience debt across systems and teams.  HAX (Human & AI Experience), conceptualized by Muthu and developed by Esperia, offers a structured approach to this shift. It integrates two complementary systems: HX (Human Experience), which preserves essential human judgment in areas like system design, data modeling, governance, and reasoning, and AX (AI Experience), which applies AI intentionally across six capability areas: detect, predict, scale, synthesize, execute, and ethics. Together, they ensure that automation enhances—not compromises—data integrity, performance, and reliability.  The framework operates through a six-stage delivery methodology spanning Discovery, Design, and Development, clarifying where human oversight is critical and where AI can safely accelerate tasks such as query optimization, migration workflows, and observability insights. HAX supports both lightweight adoption within existing DevOps pipelines and scaled implementation through reusable knowledge artifacts that standardize AI interactions across tools like ChatGPT and Gemini.

Title: Operationalizing Semantic Consistency: A DevOps Framework for Reliable Cross-Engine Analytics
Abstract: Modern data platforms increasingly depend on multiple query engines to balance performance, scalability, and workload diversity. However, SQL compatibility alone does not guarantee consistent analytical outcomes. Variations in type handling, null semantics, aggregations, and optimizer behavior often introduce subtle inconsistencies, creating challenges for data reliability, testing, and governance in Database DevOps environments.  This session presents a practical, DevOps-oriented framework for enforcing semantic consistency across heterogeneous analytics systems. By introducing analytical intent as a first-class abstraction, we demonstrate how identical queries can produce divergent results across engines such as Spark, Trino, and Snowflake. We propose a canonical semantic layer that decouples logic from execution, enabling deterministic query translation and validation.  Additionally, we outline CI/CD-integrated validation workflows, semantic testing strategies, and observability patterns that help detect and prevent drift. Attendees will learn how to embed semantic guarantees into data pipelines, improving reproducibility, trust, and operational resilience in modern data platforms.

Title: When Database Risk Stays Hidden: Closing the Visibility Gap in Data Operations
Abstract: Database DevOps is no longer limited to backups, deployments, migrations, monitoring, or performance tuning. As systems move from clusters to constellations, the real risk is often not that teams lack data; it is that critical database signals do not reach the right people in time.  This practical session reframes confidence as a measurable operational communication competency, not a personality trait. Using the VISIBLE™ Framework — Voice, Identity, Spotlight, Inner Work, Belief, Leverage, and Elevation — participants will examine how visibility gaps affect incident response, migration planning, reliability reviews, performance conversations, postmortems, and cross-functional trust.  Attendees will leave with a repeatable framework for surfacing database concerns earlier, translating technical risk into decision-ready language, and strengthening communication between DBAs, SREs, DevOps teams, engineers, product leaders, and business stakeholders. In Database DevOps, visibility is not self-promotion; it is reliability infrastructure.  By the end of this session, participants will be able to: 	1.	Identify three visibility gaps that can affect database reliability, incident response, migration risk, performance tuning, and stakeholder trust. 	2.	Apply the VISIBLE™ Framework to assess how voice, identity, spotlight, inner work, belief, leverage, and elevation influence database operations communication. 	3.	Design a practical communication strategy that helps teams surface database risks, explain tradeoffs, and move operational insight to action earlier.

Title: AI in Hiring: How Data Infrastructure Powers Smarter Talent Decisions
Abstract: Enterprise talent acquisition runs on data infrastructure, and most of it is not built for the job. Traditional applicant tracking systems were designed for administrative workflow, not for the scale, speed, or analytical demands of modern hiring operations. This session shares what it looks like to move beyond that baseline and what the data architecture decisions look like in practice.  We examine five progressive capability layers in enterprise recruiting systems, from legacy ATS deployments to self-optimizing talent pipelines, and walk through the infrastructure that makes each layer possible: skills graph frameworks, candidate matching models, vector databases, and the real-time data pipelines that feed predictive hiring workflows. These are not theoretical constructs. They are design choices with real trade-offs, and this session covers what worked, what did not, and what we would approach differently.  A significant portion of the session focuses on governance and risk. Algorithmic screening tools are under active regulatory scrutiny and litigation, including cases involving major HR platforms. We cover how bias detection, model auditing, and human oversight were built into the system and why those decisions were made, not just what was deployed.  Attendees will leave with a practical maturity framework for evaluating talent acquisition infrastructure, an understanding of the Five Layers of Predictive Talent Acquisition, and governance considerations they can apply when deploying AI in high-stakes automated decision-making.

Title: Unified DOCSIS Chipset Development: Simplifying Multi-Vendor Deployment, Interoperability, and Network Performance
Abstract: As broadband operators modernize DOCSIS networks, supporting multi-vendor environments at scale introduces significant operational complexity. Differences in chipset behavior can affect channel bonding, modulation profiles, error correction, diagnostics, and overall service reliability, making deployment and troubleshooting harder across lab, field, and production environments.  This session explores how a unified DOCSIS chipset development approach can reduce that complexity by standardizing key functions across devices and vendors. It will examine how greater consistency in upstream and downstream processing, dynamic channel management, higher-order modulation support, firmware behavior, and observability can improve interoperability and make network operations more predictable.  The presentation will also cover the practical impact of chipset standardization on validation pipelines, field trials, rollout readiness, centralized monitoring, and incident response. By reducing implementation variability, operators can streamline deployment workflows, accelerate troubleshooting, and improve coordination across engineering, operations, and field teams.  Finally, the session will connect chipset unification to longer-term network evolution, including high-split architectures, next-generation DOCSIS readiness, and more scalable broadband infrastructure. Attendees will leave with a practical framework for using chipset standardization to strengthen performance consistency, improve operational efficiency, and build a more reliable foundation for future network transformation.
Slides: Harsha Vardhani Talluri - Conf42 Database DevOps 2026.pdf

Title: Real-Time Payments for Database DevOps: Scalable, Secure Systems
Abstract: The rise of real-time payment networks such as RTP®, FedNow®, UPI, PIX, and SEPA Instant has transformed financial platforms into always-on (24/7/365), low-latency systems requiring sub-second processing and near-zero downtime. For database and DevOps engineers, this shift introduces new challenges in ensuring data consistency, resilience, and operational scalability under continuous transaction loads.  This session explores how modern real-time payment systems are engineered through the convergence of distributed databases, cloud-native architectures, and event-driven design. We examine the transition from batch processing to continuous settlement, focusing on event-streaming platforms like Apache Kafka, NATS, and RabbitMQ to enable exactly-once processing, millisecond latency, and reliable back-pressure handling. Special emphasis is placed on database concerns, including consistency models, schema evolution, and handling high-throughput transactional workloads.  We also analyze infrastructure patterns such as microservices, Kubernetes orchestration, and multi-region active-active deployments, supported by consensus algorithms like Raft and Paxos to ensure fault tolerance and data integrity. The adoption of ISO 20022 further enhances interoperability and real-time validation across global systems.  From a DevOps perspective, we cover observability, zero-trust security, and AI-driven monitoring for fraud detection and anomaly detection within strict latency constraints, while aligning with standards like PCI DSS and PSD2.  Finally, we address key challenges in database DevOps for payments: achieving ultra-low latency, maintaining strong consistency in distributed systems, and scaling reliably under unpredictable demand—offering practical insights into building resilient, data-intensive financial platforms.
Slides: Priyatham Nagaiya Seenu Naidu - Conf42 Database DevOps 2026.pdf

Title: Database DevOps at Scale: Deterministic Routing, Data Locality, and Performance Gains
Abstract: Database issues often start at the ingress layer. This talk shows how deterministic routing and DAG-based observability improve cache locality and align with data placement. Results include 41× anomaly detection, 29% lower P95 latency, and 2× throughput in production systems.  Modern database performance issues are often diagnosed at the storage or query layer—but many originate upstream at the ingress layer.  This talk presents a production-proven approach that combines deterministic routing and graph-based observability to improve data locality, cache efficiency, and overall system performance.  By routing the same entity consistently to the same pod (using consistent hashing), we restored cache locality and reduced unnecessary cross-node and cross-region calls. We further aligned routing decisions with database locality (e.g., leaseholder placement in distributed databases like CockroachDB) to minimize latency and improve consistency.  In parallel, we model API ingress pipelines as Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), where each middleware step emits structured telemetry. This enables step-level anomaly attribution, structural drift detection, and deeper visibility into request execution paths.  Production impact:  Cache hit rate improved from 70% to 94%  29% reduction in P95 latency  2× throughput without infrastructure scaling  Detection of 41× anomalies missed by traditional monitoring  The session also covers trade-offs such as hot-key amplification, load imbalance, and fallback strategies when deterministic routing is not ideal.

Title: Modern Distributed Databases: Architecture, DevOps, and Operational Trade-offs
Abstract: As organizations scale cloud-native platforms, traditional monolithic databases struggle to meet the demands of high availability, global distribution, and real-time data processing. This session explores the evolution of distributed database architectures and the critical role DevOps practices play in building, deploying, and operating resilient data platforms at scale.  We will examine foundational concepts such as the CAP theorem, replication models, sharding strategies, and consensus protocols like Raft and Paxos that enable modern distributed databases to maintain availability, performance, and fault tolerance. The talk will also highlight how DevOps-driven automation through infrastructure as code, configuration management, and continuous deployment improves database reliability, scalability, and operational efficiency in production environments.  Additionally, the session will address modern security and governance practices including zero-trust infrastructure, certificate-based authentication, and distributed key management. Attendees will gain practical insights into managing distributed databases through DevOps methodologies, optimizing operational workflows, and building secure, scalable, and highly automated data platforms for modern enterprise and cloud-native applications.

Title: Keeping Databases Always Available: Active-Active DNS Strategies for DevOps and Failover
Abstract: Database availability is critical for modern applications, yet DNS is often an overlooked dependency that can delay failover and disrupt connectivity during outages. Traditional single-provider and failover-based DNS models introduce latency, stale records, and operational complexity that directly impact database resilience.  This talk explores how an Active-Active, multi-provider DNS architecture can improve database availability in DevOps-driven environments. By running multiple DNS providers simultaneously, systems can route traffic in real time based on health checks and endpoint availability ensuring faster failover for primary/replica databases and minimizing downtime.  We’ll examine how DNS integrates with database replication, failover automation, and global read/write routing strategies. Attendees will learn practical approaches to synchronizing configurations, reducing recovery time, and improving observability across distributed systems.  This session is ideal for engineers managing databases at scale who want to strengthen reliability, optimize failover, and align DNS strategy with modern DevOps practices.
